title: The Best Movies at Telluride and Venice, and the 10 Most Anticipated Fall Films
format: Amanda phones in from Venice and Sean reports from Telluride: each ranks their five festival favorites, they build a combined ten most-anticipated fall list, take a first pass at the Best Picture race, and Sean talks to 'Lurker' writer-director Alex Russell.

films.title: A House of Dynamite
films.note: No. 1 - Kathryn Bigelow's first film in eight years, a taut political thriller for Netflix under two hours. Amanda saw it an hour before recording and was riveted; Tracy Letts name-drops Francisco Lindor in it.

films.title: No Other Choice
films.note: No. 2 - Park Chan-wook's late-capitalism satire, 2.5 hours, which she called the festival's leader until 'House of Dynamite.' Her pick for the Golden Lion.

films.title: The Testament of Ann Lee
films.note: No. 3 - Mona Fastvold's Shaker-founder film, written with Brady Corbett, starring Amanda Seyfried; a full-on musical built from traditional Shaker music, scored by Daniel Blumberg. A surprise for her.

films.title: Bugonia
films.note: No. 4 - Yorgos Lanthimos adapting the 2003 Korean film 'Save the Green Planet,' written by Will Tracy, with Emma Stone and Jesse Plemons. Her preferred Lanthimos flavor; the Plemons performance is a Best Actor case.

films.title: Megadoc
films.note: No. 5 - Mike Figgis's on-set making-of 'Megalopolis,' which answers some of the questions Coppola's film raised. Figgis steps in front of the camera; Shia LaBeouf and Aubrey Plaza feature heavily.

segments.heading: Also from Venice
segments.note: honorable mentions and the walkout that wasn't

films.title: Jay Kelly
films.note: Noah Baumbach's sincere, un-bitter film about an aging star (George Clooney), his business manager (Adam Sandler), and publicist (Laura Dern). Mixed reviews out of Venice; played much better at Telluride, where Sean saw it. He calls it Baumbach's most beautiful-looking film, shot by Linus Sandgren.

films.title: Marc by Sofia
films.note: Sofia Coppola's Marc Jacobs documentary for A24. Amanda: beautiful to look at, zero tension by design. 'It's not Hearts of Darkness.' Coppola picked A24 because 'they'll let me do it how I want.'

films.title: After the Hunt
films.note: Luca Guadagnino's cancel-culture drama with Julia Roberts, Andrew Garfield, and Ayo Edebiri; opens with a Woody Allen-font title card. Out of competition. Amanda had to leave early for 'Jay Kelly' after a rain-soaked, late-starting screening. 'It's Tár, but we already have Tár.'

films.title: Sentimental Value
films.note: No. 1 - Joachim Trier's Cannes Grand Prix winner with Renate Reinsve, Stellan Skarsgård, and Elle Fanning; an emotional continuation of 'The Worst Person in the World.' Sean texted people this is the Best Picture winner.

films.title: The Secret Agent
films.note: No. 2 - Kleber Mendonça Filho's 1977-set character study and espionage thriller with Wagner Moura. Sean rates it far above 'I'm Still Here'; comps it to 'The Parallax View' and 'All the President's Men.' Neon.

films.title: Hamnet
films.note: No. 3 - Chloé Zhao's adaptation of Maggie O'Farrell's novel, Paul Mescal as Shakespeare and Jessie Buckley as Agnes. The talk of the festival; a period weepy with a thrilling final 20 minutes. Sean's Best Actress pick for Buckley.

films.title: Bugonia
films.note: No. 4 - same as Amanda's No. 4. Modest in scale, more or less a three-hander; Stavros Halkias is very funny in it.

films.title: It Was Just an Accident
films.note: No. 5 - Jafar Panahi's Palme d'Or winner (Neon), his most commercial film: a thriller about mistaken identity with one of the year's best endings. Panahi was a medallion recipient at the festival.

segments.heading: Everything else Sean saw at Telluride
segments.note: the highlights, the docs, and the letdowns

films.title: Cover-Up
films.note: Laura Poitras and Mark Obenhaus's documentary on investigative journalist Seymour Hersh, who broke the My Lai Massacre story. Just outside Sean's top five; interviewed by Ezra Edelman, who grew up two doors down from Hersh.

films.title: Pillion
films.note: Cannes film with Alexander Skarsgård and Harry Melling: a very graphic BDSM rom-com, 'probably the first since Exit to Eden.' Funny and sweet, it was a big hit.

films.title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
films.note: Mary Bronstein's harrowing portrait of motherhood, Rose Byrne's face on camera for nearly the whole film. Very Safdies-inflected (Ronald Bronstein edits their films). Byrne in the Best Actress conversation.

films.title: The Mastermind
films.note: Kelly Reichardt's '70s arthouse thriller with Josh O'Connor, who sat next to Sean at the screening. Quiet and downbeat, classic Reichardt.

films.title: Blue Moon
films.note: Richard Linklater's Lorenz Hart film; Ethan Hawke at the end of Hart's life at Sardi's. One of Sean's favorite performances of the year, very stage-like.

films.title: Nouvelle Vague
films.note: Linklater's other Telluride film, about the making of 'Breathless.' Sean clicked with it less: 'this happened, then this happened' rather than an emotional arc.

films.title: Tuner
films.note: Documentarian Daniel Roher's ('Navalny') first feature: Leo Woodall as a piano tuner with hypersensitive hearing that also makes him a great safecracker. A musical drama that turns into a heist thriller. The surprise of the festival.

films.title: Man on the Run
films.note: Morgan Neville's Paul McCartney documentary, the film Sean most regrets missing (it ran opposite 'Jay Kelly').

films.title: Frankenstein
films.note: Guillermo del Toro's Mary Shelley adaptation, Oscar Isaac as the doctor, Jacob Elordi as the monster, Mia Goth in two parts. Faithful but turgid; Sean was taken aback by how much CGI and how visually poor it looked.

films.title: Springsteen: Deliver Me from Nowhere
films.note: Scott Cooper's biopic of the making of 'Nebraska,' Jeremy Allen White as Bruce (borderline remarkable, nails the singing), Jeremy Strong as manager Jon Landau. Sean found the movie itself juiceless.

films.title: Ballad of a Small Player
films.note: Edward Berger's Macau gambler drama with Colin Farrell. On paper a Sean movie, but the script is no good: 'five different movies at once.' A quiet, tough Friday-night premiere.

films.title: La Grazia
films.note: Paolo Sorrentino's film about a president (Toni Servillo) in his final days in office, with a techno/hip-hop motif. Opened Venice; was the Telluride patron screening. Sean found it dull: buildup, no delivery.

films.title: The Wizard of the Kremlin
films.note: Olivier Assayas's film from a French novel, Jude Law as Putin, Paul Dano as his fixer, Alicia Vikander and Jeffrey Wright. Sean was 20 minutes late; a fun time but not a successful movie.

segments.heading: The 10 Most Anticipated Fall Films
segments.note: two top-fives that share two titles

picks.title: One Battle After Another
picks.note: Paul Thomas Anderson's, unseen by either, out in three weeks. 'They always come through for PTA.'

picks.title: Marty Supreme
picks.note: Josh Safdie's table-tennis film with Timothée Chalamet (1950s) and Gwyneth Paltrow; Sean has watched the trailer six times. A24's Christmas release.

picks.title: Sentimental Value
picks.note: Sean's Telluride No. 1, which Amanda has yet to see.

picks.title: Hamnet
picks.note: The Telluride breakout; Amanda anticipating from afar.

picks.title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
picks.note: The Rose Byrne motherhood film: she's nervous about it but wants the challenge.

picks.title: A House of Dynamite
picks.note: Amanda's Venice No. 1, which Sean would like to see soon.

picks.title: No Other Choice
picks.note: The Park Chan-wook film Amanda's pumped for; Sean has already seen it.

picks.title: The Testament of Ann Lee
picks.note: Amanda's Venice surprise; Sean anticipating the Amanda Seyfried Oscar campaign.

segments.heading: The Best Picture Top 10, for now
segments.note: a first, admittedly fake, pass at the race
segments.subject: Best Picture

films.title: Sentimental Value
films.note: No. 1 - Sean's early call, the way he called 'Anora.'

films.title: Hamnet
films.note: No. 2 - Focus's prized pony; the festival consensus movie.

films.title: A House of Dynamite
films.note: No. 3 - though they doubt Netflix wins with it after already doing 'the Bigelow thing.'

films.title: Marty Supreme
films.note: No. 4 - A24 never lacks a contender; Chalamet, Paltrow, a period piece.

films.title: No Other Choice
films.note: No. 5 - Neon, and maybe the year Park Chan-wook breaks into Best Picture.

films.title: It Was Just an Accident
films.note: No. 6 - the Palme d'Or winner, Neon behind it.

films.title: One Battle After Another
films.note: No. 7 - unseen, but they always come through for PTA.

films.title: Sinners
films.note: No. 8 - already out and a hit; near-certain nominee.

films.title: Wicked for Good
films.note: No. 9 - the head-down Hollywood lock.

films.title: Jay Kelly
films.note: No. 10 - a lot of weight behind it; the Sandler campaign could go far.

films.title: Lurker
films.note: Russell's feature debut, a music-industry thriller he wrote during COVID out of a feeling of disgust at male social hierarchy. A TV writer ('The Bear,' 'Beef') with ~8 days of set experience before shooting, he cast on how well people understood the script. Cited references: 'Whiplash,' 'Nightcrawler,' and 'Almost Famous'; DP Pat Scola ('Sing Sing') brought warmth over a cold Fincher look.

If I Had Legs I'd Kick You.title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
